title: Climate change — why the planet is warming and what it means
examTip: AQA Geography Paper 1, Topic 1 (The Challenge of Natural Hazards). Climate change questions can be worth up to 9 marks. You must know the difference between natural and human causes, the evidence for climate change, and the distinction between mitigation and adaptation strategies. This topic also links to coasts, rivers, and development.
introTips: Covers both the evidence for climate change and its natural and human causes
You'll be able to evaluate the effects of climate change on people and environments
By the end you can compare mitigation and adaptation strategies — a frequent 6-mark question
intro: Climate change is one of the most significant challenges facing every country on Earth — and it shows up in almost every topic in AQA Geography, from coastal erosion to global development. Understanding why the climate is changing, what the consequences are, and what we can realistically do about it will help you in Paper 1 and beyond. This is also a topic where the science directly shapes real political decisions.

keyPoints.heading: Evidence for climate change
keyPoints.content: Before looking at causes, it helps to know what the evidence actually shows. Temperature records going back to the 1880s show that average global temperatures have risen by approximately 0.85°C (some sources now state over 1.1°C above pre-industrial levels). Retreating glaciers are visible worldwide — Glacier National Park in Montana had 150 glaciers in 1910; fewer than 30 remain. Rising sea levels have increased by about 20 cm since 1900, and the rate is accelerating. Ice cores drilled from Antarctic ice contain trapped air bubbles giving us 800,000 years of atmospheric CO₂ and temperature data — showing a tight correlation between greenhouse gas levels and global temperature. Species migration — animals and plants are shifting their ranges poleward or to higher altitudes as conditions change. It is important to distinguish between weather (short-term atmospheric conditions — what's happening today) and climate (the long-term average pattern over 30+ years). A cold winter does not disprove climate change — you need to look at long-term trends.
writeBullets: Temperature records — global average has risen ~0.85–1.1°C since pre-industrial times
Retreating glaciers — Glacier National Park: 150 glaciers in 1910, fewer than 30 now
Sea level rise — ~20 cm since 1900, and rate is accelerating
Ice cores — 800,000 years of CO₂ and temperature data; show tight correlation
Species migration — ranges shifting poleward/to higher altitude as temperatures rise
Weather vs climate — weather is short-term; climate is long-term average (30+ years)

keyTerms.def: Long-term shifts in global temperatures and weather patterns, now primarily driven by human activity
A cylinder of ice drilled from glaciers or ice sheets, containing records of past atmospheric conditions
Short-term atmospheric conditions (temperature, rainfall, wind) at a particular place and time
The long-term average pattern of weather in a region, calculated over 30+ years

keyPoints.memTip: Evidence acronym: TRISS — Temperature records, Retreating glaciers, Ice cores, Sea level rise, Species migration. Cover your notes and explain each piece of evidence with one specific statistic or example.
keyPoints.tutor_note: When asked to 'describe the evidence for climate change', name specific evidence types and include data — not just 'temperatures are rising'. Examiners reward precision: '0.85°C rise since 1880' is worth more than 'it is getting hotter'. Don't confuse weather with climate — that distinction comes up in exam questions.

keyPoints.heading: Natural causes of climate change
keyPoints.content: Climate has always changed throughout Earth's history, long before humans existed. The main natural mechanisms are Milankovitch cycles — slow, predictable changes in Earth's orbit and tilt. These include: eccentricity (the shape of Earth's orbit changes from more circular to more elliptical over ~100,000 years, altering how much solar energy Earth receives); axial tilt (the angle of Earth's tilt varies between 22.1° and 24.5° over ~41,000 years, affecting seasonal extremes); and precession (the 'wobble' of Earth's rotational axis, like a spinning top, over ~26,000 years, shifting which hemisphere gets more summer sun). Milankovitch cycles explain ice ages — but they operate over tens of thousands of years, far too slowly to explain the rapid warming of the last 150 years. Solar output varies in 11-year cycles (sunspot cycles) — more sunspot activity = slightly more solar energy reaching Earth. But solar output alone cannot explain current warming. Volcanic activity releases aerosols (fine particles) that reflect sunlight back to space, causing short-term cooling. For example, the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo temporarily lowered global temperatures by ~0.5°C for 2 years.
writeBullets: Milankovitch cycles — three orbital variations: eccentricity (~100,000 yrs), axial tilt (~41,000 yrs), precession (~26,000 yrs)
Eccentricity — orbit changes from circular to elliptical; affects total solar energy received
Axial tilt — angle of tilt varies (22.1–24.5°); affects strength of seasons
Precession — wobble of rotational axis; shifts which hemisphere faces sun in summer
Solar output — 11-year sunspot cycles cause slight variation in solar energy reaching Earth
Volcanic aerosols — fine particles reflect sunlight; cause short-term cooling (e.g. Pinatubo 1991)

keyTerms.def: Slow, predictable changes in Earth's orbit and tilt that cause long-term climate variation over thousands of years
The changing shape of Earth's orbit from circular to elliptical over ~100,000 years
The varying angle of Earth's rotational axis (22.1–24.5°) over ~41,000 years
The wobble of Earth's rotational axis over ~26,000 years, like a spinning top
Fine particles ejected by volcanoes that reflect sunlight and cause short-term cooling

keyPoints.memTip: Milankovitch's three cycles: 'Every Ant Precedes' — Eccentricity, Axial tilt, Precession. Cover your notes and explain each Milankovitch cycle, its timescale, and the mechanism by which it changes climate.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Examiners will ask you to distinguish between natural and human causes. The key argument is timescale: natural causes operate over thousands to millions of years and cannot explain the rapid warming of the last 150 years. The current rate of warming is far faster than any natural cycle — that's why scientists attribute it primarily to human activity.

keyPoints.heading: Human causes: the enhanced greenhouse effect
keyPoints.content: The greenhouse effect is a natural process — without it, Earth's average temperature would be around -18°C rather than +15°C. It works like this: the Sun emits shortwave radiation (visible light) that passes through the atmosphere and warms the Earth's surface. The warmed surface emits longwave (infrared) radiation back upward. Greenhouse gases in the atmosphere absorb this longwave radiation and re-emit it in all directions, including back toward Earth — trapping heat. The natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth warm enough for life. The problem is the enhanced greenhouse effect: human activities are increasing the concentration of greenhouse gases, trapping more heat than before. Key greenhouse gases and their sources: CO₂ — burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) and deforestation (fewer trees to absorb CO₂); methane (CH₄) — agriculture (livestock, rice paddies), landfill waste decomposition; nitrous oxide (N₂O) — fertilisers and agriculture; water vapour — a feedback effect (warmer air holds more water vapour, which amplifies warming). The IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) — the world's leading body of climate scientists — concludes with over 95% confidence that current warming is primarily caused by human activity.
writeBullets: Greenhouse effect — natural process; shortwave radiation in, longwave radiation trapped; keeps Earth at +15°C
Enhanced greenhouse effect — human activities increase greenhouse gas concentrations, trapping more heat
CO₂ — burning fossil fuels; deforestation (less CO₂ absorbed)
Methane — livestock digestion, rice paddies, landfill decomposition
Nitrous oxide — fertilisers and agricultural soils
IPCC — consensus: >95% confidence that current warming is primarily human-caused

keyTerms.def: The natural process by which greenhouse gases trap longwave radiation from Earth's surface, warming the planet
The increased trapping of heat due to rising greenhouse gas concentrations from human activity
The most significant human-emitted greenhouse gas; produced by burning fossil fuels and deforestation
A potent greenhouse gas produced by livestock, rice farming, and landfill decomposition
Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change; the international body that assesses climate science

keyPoints.memTip: Greenhouse gases: 'Can Monkeys Need Water?' — CO₂, Methane, Nitrous oxide, Water vapour. Cover your notes and explain the mechanism of the enhanced greenhouse effect in four steps: shortwave in → surface warms → longwave out → gases trap and re-emit.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Many students describe the greenhouse effect as 'CO₂ blocking the Sun'. That's wrong — CO₂ is transparent to incoming shortwave radiation. It only absorbs and re-emits the outgoing longwave (infrared) radiation from the Earth's surface. Getting the mechanism right will earn you marks that vague descriptions won't.

keyPoints.heading: Effects of climate change on people and environments
keyPoints.content: Climate change is already affecting every region of the world, and the effects will intensify. Sea level rise is caused by two processes: thermal expansion (water expands as it warms) and ice melt (glaciers and ice sheets add water to the oceans). Low-lying countries face existential threats — the Maldives has an average elevation of just 1.5 metres; Bangladesh has 17 million people living below 1 metre above sea level. Extreme weather is becoming more frequent and intense: more powerful tropical storms (warmer seas = more energy), longer droughts, more intense heatwaves, and more extreme rainfall events. Biodiversity loss: coral reefs bleach when sea temperatures rise just 1–2°C above normal — the Great Barrier Reef has experienced mass bleaching events. Species that cannot migrate fast enough face extinction. Food and water insecurity: changing rainfall patterns disrupt agriculture; glaciers that supply freshwater to millions of people are retreating. Mass migration: rising seas, desertification, and extreme heat make some regions uninhabitable, potentially displacing hundreds of millions of people by 2050 — creating climate refugees.
writeBullets: Sea level rise — thermal expansion of water + ice melt; threatens Maldives (~1.5m avg elevation), Bangladesh
More extreme weather — stronger tropical storms, longer droughts, more intense rainfall
Coral bleaching — sea temps rising 1–2°C causes mass bleaching; Great Barrier Reef severely affected
Food insecurity — shifting rainfall patterns disrupt farming; glacial freshwater retreating
Biodiversity loss — species unable to migrate fast enough face extinction
Climate refugees — displacement of millions as seas rise and regions become uninhabitable

keyTerms.def: The rise in sea level caused by water expanding in volume as it warms
The loss of algae from coral when sea temperatures rise, causing corals to turn white and die
A person forced to leave their home due to the effects of climate change
Lack of reliable access to sufficient, safe, nutritious food — worsened by climate change

keyPoints.memTip: Effects of climate change: 'SEBFM' — Sea level rise, Extreme weather, Biodiversity loss, Food/water insecurity, Migration. Cover your notes and give a specific named example for at least three of these effects.
keyPoints.tutor_note: For the sea level rise question, always mention BOTH mechanisms — thermal expansion AND ice melt — not just one. Both earn separate marks. For effects, link to specific vulnerable places (Maldives, Bangladesh) rather than speaking in generalities.

keyPoints.heading: Responses: mitigation vs adaptation
keyPoints.content: Responses to climate change fall into two categories. Mitigation means reducing greenhouse gas emissions to slow or stop climate change from getting worse. Examples include: switching to renewable energy (solar, wind, hydroelectric — no CO₂ emitted during generation); improving energy efficiency (insulating buildings, electric vehicles); reforestation (planting trees to absorb CO₂ as carbon sinks); international agreements — the Paris Agreement 2015 committed nearly 200 countries to limiting warming to 1.5–2°C above pre-industrial levels by reducing emissions. Adaptation means adjusting to the changes that are already happening or are inevitable. Examples include: building sea walls and flood barriers (Thames Barrier, Netherlands' Delta Works); developing drought-resistant crops for drier climates; changing farming practices (shifting planting seasons, different crop varieties); managed retreat (relocating communities away from flood-prone coastlines). Both are needed — mitigation alone cannot prevent all warming already locked in; adaptation alone does not tackle the root cause. Action happens at multiple scales: individual (changing diet, reducing air travel), national (energy policy, building regulations), and international (Paris Agreement, IPCC reports).
writeBullets: Mitigation — reducing greenhouse gas emissions to slow climate change
Renewable energy — solar, wind, hydro replace fossil fuels; key mitigation strategy
Paris Agreement 2015 — ~200 countries commit to limiting warming to 1.5–2°C
Reforestation — trees absorb CO₂; helps restore carbon sinks
Adaptation — adjusting to climate change already happening or inevitable
Sea walls / flood barriers — protect coastal communities (e.g. Thames Barrier)
Drought-resistant crops — adapt farming to drier, less predictable conditions
Both needed — mitigation slows the problem; adaptation manages unavoidable change

keyTerms.def: Actions that reduce greenhouse gas emissions to slow or stop climate change
Adjusting systems and behaviours to cope with the effects of climate change already occurring
2015 international treaty committing nearly 200 countries to limit global warming to 1.5–2°C
A natural system (e.g. forests, oceans) that absorbs more CO₂ than it releases
The planned relocation of communities away from flood or erosion risk areas

keyPoints.memTip: Mitigation = Make it stop (reduce emissions). Adaptation = Adjust to live with it. Cover your notes and give three specific examples of each, then explain which scale of action each operates at (individual, national, or international).
keyPoints.tutor_note: The single most common mistake in this topic is confusing mitigation and adaptation. Mitigation targets the CAUSE (reducing CO₂). Adaptation targets the EFFECTS (dealing with what's already happening). For 6-mark questions, always give multiple examples of each AND explain the reasoning — not just a list.

tables.title: Natural vs human causes of climate change
rows: Orbital shape changes; varies solar energy received
Angle of tilt varies; affects seasonal extremes
Wobble of Earth's axis; shifts summer solar input
Sunspot cycles vary energy reaching Earth
Partially, but too small to explain observed warming
Fine particles reflect sunlight; short-term cooling
No — causes cooling, not long-term warming
Burning coal, oil, gas releases CO₂; enhanced greenhouse effect
Fewer trees to absorb CO₂; also releases stored carbon
Livestock, rice paddies, landfill emit methane (25× more potent than CO₂)

tables.title: Mitigation vs adaptation: strategies and examples
rows: Replaces fossil fuels; reduces CO₂ emissions
Commits ~200 countries to emission reduction targets
Trees absorb CO₂; restores carbon sinks
Reduces energy demand and fossil fuel use
Protects coastal communities from rising seas
Maintains food production in drier conditions
Relocates communities away from flood risk areas
Adjusts farming to new temperature and rainfall patterns

1.question: Why can Milankovitch cycles NOT explain the rapid warming observed since around 1850?
options: Milankovitch cycles only cause cooling, not warming
Milankovitch cycles operate over tens to hundreds of thousands of years — far too slowly to explain warming over 150 years
Milankovitch cycles were disproved by the IPCC in 2015
Milankovitch cycles only affect the southern hemisphere
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Milankovitch cycles are real and do cause climate change — but they operate over 26,000 to 100,000 year timescales. The warming since 1850 (about 150 years) is far too rapid to be explained by orbital cycles. That rapid rate points to human causes.
1.wrongFeedback: The key issue is timescale. Milankovitch cycles are real — they explain ice ages over thousands to hundreds of thousands of years. But the rapid warming since the Industrial Revolution (~1850) has happened in just 150 years. No natural orbital cycle operates that fast. The timescale mismatch is strong evidence for human causes.

4.question: What is the difference between mitigation and adaptation in response to climate change?
options: Mitigation deals with the effects of climate change; adaptation deals with the causes
Mitigation means building sea walls; adaptation means planting trees
Mitigation reduces greenhouse gas emissions to slow climate change; adaptation adjusts to the changes already happening
They are two terms for the same set of strategies
4.correctFeedback: Correct. Mitigation targets the cause — reducing the greenhouse gas emissions that drive climate change. Adaptation targets the effects — adjusting how we live and build to cope with the changes that are already locked in.
4.wrongFeedback: This is a very commonly tested distinction. Mitigation = tackling the CAUSE (reducing emissions — e.g. renewable energy, Paris Agreement, reforestation). Adaptation = adjusting to the EFFECTS already happening (e.g. sea walls, drought-resistant crops, managed retreat). Not the other way around.

commonMistakes: Confusing mitigation and adaptation — mitigation targets the cause of climate change (reducing greenhouse gas emissions); adaptation targets the effects (adjusting to changes that are already happening). These must not be swapped.
Saying CO₂ blocks sunlight coming in — it doesn't. CO₂ is transparent to shortwave (incoming solar) radiation. It absorbs and re-emits the outgoing longwave (infrared) radiation from Earth's surface.

Claiming Milankovitch cycles explain current warming — they do cause climate change but over tens of thousands of years. The rapid warming since 1850 is far too fast for any natural orbital mechanism.
Listing only thermal expansion or only ice melt for sea level rise — both processes contribute and both are worth separate marks. Always name both mechanisms.
Giving generic effects without named locations — 'low-lying areas will flood' earns fewer marks than 'the Maldives (average elevation 1.5m) faces an existential threat from sea level rise'.
examTips: For 'explain the evidence for climate change' questions, name specific types of evidence (temperature records, ice cores, retreating glaciers, sea level rise, species migration) and include data — not just general statements.
The mitigation vs adaptation distinction is almost always assessed. Learn clear definitions and at least three examples of each, at different scales (individual, national, international).

When explaining the enhanced greenhouse effect, describe the mechanism in steps: shortwave radiation in → surface warms → longwave radiation emitted → greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit → surface warms further. Don't just say 'CO₂ traps heat'.

For effects of climate change, use the structure: physical effect → who/what is affected → named example. E.g. 'Sea level rise will threaten the Maldives, where average elevation is just 1.5 metres, potentially displacing the entire population.'

In 9-mark extended writing questions on climate change, you must reach a clear and justified conclusion. 'Both natural and human causes play a role, but human causes dominate because of the speed of change' is the kind of evaluative conclusion that reaches Level 3.
revisionCardBullets: Evidence: temperature records (+0.85°C since 1880), retreating glaciers, sea level rise (+20cm since 1900), ice cores, species migration
Weather = short-term atmospheric conditions; climate = long-term average pattern (30+ years)
Milankovitch cycles: eccentricity (~100,000 yrs), axial tilt (~41,000 yrs), precession (~26,000 yrs)
Solar output — 11-year sunspot cycles; small variation in solar energy
Volcanic aerosols — reflect sunlight; short-term cooling (Pinatubo 1991 = -0.5°C for 2 years)
Greenhouse effect: shortwave in → surface warms → longwave out → greenhouse gases trap and re-emit
Key greenhouse gases: CO₂ (fossil fuels, deforestation), methane (livestock, landfill), nitrous oxide (fertilisers)
IPCC: >95% confidence current warming is primarily human-caused
Sea level rise: thermal expansion + ice melt; Maldives (1.5m avg) and Bangladesh most at risk
Mitigation = reduce emissions (renewable energy, Paris Agreement 2015, reforestation)
Adaptation = adjust to effects (sea walls, drought-resistant crops, managed retreat, changed farming)
Both mitigation and adaptation are needed — mitigation slows the cause; adaptation manages unavoidable change
